questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (metformin + dapagliflozin) has 2 days of nausea, vomiting, and malaise during febrile pyelonephritis. She is tachypneic with deep sighing breaths. Glucose 12.6 mmol/L, Na 134, Cl 96, HCO₃ 9, serum ketones strongly positive. Which statement best explains her presentation?
options: Metformin accumulation produced a high-anion-gap lactic acidosis
Her SGLT2 inhibitor kept glucose near-normal while counter-regulatory hormones drove unchecked ketogenesis
The infection caused a non-anion-gap acidosis from bicarbonate loss
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state from inadequate insulin secretion
questions.explain: Euglycemic DKA — a hallmark SGLT2-inhibitor risk during acute stress. Anion gap = 134−(96+9)=29 with ketonemia, yet glucose is only 12.6 because glucosuria masks the hyperglycemia. Pyelonephritis drove a counter-regulatory surge (glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine) → lipolysis and ketogenesis.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with T2DM, HbA1c 8.6%, BMI 37, wants a second-line agent that will also help him lose weight before knee surgery. eGFR 78. Which is most appropriate to add to metformin?
questions.explain: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide) give potent A1c lowering plus substantial weight loss via central satiety. Sulfonylureas cause weight gain and hypoglycemia; DPP-4 inhibitors and acarbose are weight-neutral with weaker efficacy.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with T2DM and hypertension on metformin and ramipril 10 mg has BP 124/76. Labs (2 weeks after starting ramipril): HbA1c 7.4%, K 4.4, creatinine 118 µmol/L (baseline 92). What is the best next step?
options: Stop ramipril for acute kidney injury
Continue ramipril at the current dose and monitor
questions.explain: Creatinine rose ~28% (26/92), within the accepted ≤30% rise expected when an ACEi lowers intraglomerular pressure, and K+ is normal → continue and monitor. Switch to an ARB only for true ACEi cough or angioedema.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with T2DM and stable ischemic heart disease has BP 122/74, HbA1c 6.8%. Lipids: total chol 6.2, TG 1.8, HDL 0.9, LDL 3.1 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate initial step for his lipids?
questions.explain: Diabetes + established macrovascular disease mandates a statin; the validated target is LDL < 2.0 mmol/L (or ≥50% reduction). Fibrates are for severe hypertriglyceridemia; ezetimibe is an add-on, not first-line.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old with T1DM is found unresponsive. Glucose 34.0 mmol/L, Na 130, Cl 93, HCO₃ 6, K 2.9 mmol/L; ABG confirms severe metabolic acidosis. Normal saline is running. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: IV bolus of 10 units regular insulin
IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidemia
Start an IV regular insulin infusion at 0.1 u/kg/h
Give IV potassium chloride and withhold insulin
questions.explain: K+ is critically low (2.9). Insulin would shift K+ intracellularly and risk fatal arrhythmia. If K+ < 3.3, hold insulin and replace potassium first, then start insulin once K+ ≥ 3.3.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with T2DM (metformin + sitagliptin) admitted for a non-healing neuropathic toe ulcer has eGFR drop to 24 (baseline 52). Random glucose 16.4 mmol/L. Which adjustment is most appropriate?
options: Stop metformin and sitagliptin; start subcutaneous basal insulin
Reduce metformin to 500 mg and keep sitagliptin
Stop metformin, switch sitagliptin to linagliptin, keep monitoring
Keep metformin, stop sitagliptin, add canagliflozin
questions.explain: eGFR < 30 contraindicates metformin (lactic acidosis). With a severe active infection and marked hyperglycemia, oral agents are insufficient — insulin is the safest, most titratable choice. (Linagliptin would be renally fine, but won't control this degree of hyperglycemia.)

questions.stem: An asymptomatic 50-year-old has an incidental HbA1c of 7.1%. What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Diagnose diabetes now and start metformin
Repeat a confirmatory glycemic test on another day
Reassure; a single A1c cannot suggest diabetes
questions.explain: In an asymptomatic person, one abnormal test needs a confirmatory repeat (same or different assay) on a separate day. A single result is diagnostic only with classic symptoms or a hyperglycemic crisis.

questions.stem: A man with sickle cell trait has fasting glucoses of 7.4 and 7.6 mmol/L but an HbA1c of 5.7%. Why is his A1c discordantly low?
options: Sickle cell shortens RBC lifespan, falsely lowering A1c
He has occult B12 deficiency raising glucose
Fasting glucose is unreliable in hemoglobinopathy
A1c of 5.7% rules out diabetes regardless of glucose
questions.explain: Conditions that shorten RBC lifespan (hemolysis, sickle cell, thalassemia, recent transfusion) reduce glycation time and falsely lower A1c. Use fasting glucose/OGTT — his fasting values are diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with T2DM and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction has HbA1c 7.6% on metformin, eGFR 55. Which add-on best matches his comorbidity?
questions.explain: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce heart-failure hospitalizations and give renal protection — the comorbidity-driven choice in HFrEF. Saxagliptin is specifically associated with increased HF hospitalization; sulfonylureas add hypoglycemia/weight gain.

questions.stem: Which oral agent is cleared non-renally and needs no dose adjustment even in end-stage renal disease?
questions.explain: Linagliptin is excreted largely via the bile/gut, so it needs no renal dose adjustment and is safe in ESRD. Sitagliptin/saxagliptin need reductions; metformin and glyburide are stopped in advanced CKD.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old with T2DM has a random UACR of 4.5 mg/mmol. He had a UTI treated last week. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: Diagnose diabetic nephropathy and start an ACEi today
Repeat UACR — confirm 2 of 3 samples over ~3 months before labelling nephropathy
Ignore it; UACR this low is always normal
questions.explain: A single elevated UACR never diagnoses nephropathy — confirm 2 of 3 over ~3 months. Recent UTI, exercise, fever, HF, and menstruation transiently raise UACR. (An ACEi is appropriate once albuminuria is confirmed.)

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed 45-year-old with T2DM has no visual symptoms. When should retinopathy screening begin?
questions.explain: Because T2DM may have been present for years before diagnosis, screen with a dilated fundus exam at diagnosis, then q1–2 yr. (T1DM: 5 years after onset, age ≥15, then annually.)

questions.stem: During DKA treatment a patient's glucose falls to 13 mmol/L but the anion gap is still 18 and ketones remain positive. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Stop the insulin infusion now that glucose is near-normal
Continue insulin and add IV D5W
Give an insulin bolus and stop fluids
Switch immediately to subcutaneous basal insulin
questions.explain: The endpoint is anion-gap closure, not glucose. Stopping insulin now lets ketoacidosis rebound. Continue the infusion and add dextrose (D5W) to keep glucose up until the gap closes.

guide.s: treat the gap not the glucose

questions.stem: An 80-year-old with T2DM is confused and severely dehydrated. Glucose 41 mmol/L, HCO₃ 23, anion gap 10, ketones negative, calculated osmolality 335 mOsm/kg. Which best fits?
questions.explain: HHS: extreme hyperglycemia and hyperosmolality with minimal ketosis/acidosis (normal bicarbonate, normal gap, negative ketones). Residual insulin suppresses lipolysis but can't control glucose. Cornerstone of treatment is aggressive fluids.

questions.stem: A frail 84-year-old with T2DM, HbA1c 7.9%, recurrent falls, and eGFR 40 needs a second agent. Hypoglycemia must be avoided. Best choice?
questions.explain: In the frail elderly where hypoglycemia and renal dosing matter most, a DPP-4 inhibitor is ideal — weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia. Linagliptin needs no renal adjustment. Sulfonylureas (esp. glyburide) are the wrong choice here.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old with T2DM develops a persistent dry cough 3 weeks after starting ramipril. K 4.6, creatinine stable. What is the most appropriate change?
options: Add a cough suppressant and continue ramipril
questions.explain: ACEi cough is bradykinin-mediated. Switch to an ARB (candesartan), which preserves renoprotection without the cough. Don't abandon cardiorenal protection altogether.

questions.stem: A patient on metformin reports metallic taste, and develops a macrocytic anemia over 2 years. Which adverse effect explains the anemia?
questions.explain: Long-term metformin causes vitamin B12 malabsorption → macrocytic anemia (and can worsen neuropathy). Check B12 in metformin users with macrocytosis or neuropathy.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old with long-standing T2DM has early satiety, bloating, and erratic glucose readings with unexplained post-meal hypoglycemia. Which complication best explains this?
questions.explain: Autonomic neuropathy → gastroparesis: delayed, unpredictable gastric emptying uncouples insulin timing from carbohydrate absorption, causing erratic glucose and post-prandial hypoglycemia.

questions.stem: A patient on atorvastatin is started on clarithromycin for pneumonia and develops severe diffuse myalgia with a markedly elevated CK. What is the mechanism?
options: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, raising atorvastatin levels and myopathy risk
Statins and macrolides both deplete coenzyme Q10 additively
The pneumonia caused rhabdomyolysis independent of drugs
questions.explain: Atorvastatin is CYP3A4-metabolized; clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor → higher statin levels → myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Rosuvastatin (fewer CYP3A4 interactions) or holding the statin during the course is safer.

questions.stem: A DKA patient is now alert, eating, with a closed anion gap and normal bicarbonate. The team plans to stop the IV insulin infusion. What must happen to prevent relapse?
options: Stop the infusion immediately; the gap is closed
Overlap: give subcutaneous basal insulin and stop the IV insulin ~4 hours later
Stop insulin and recheck ketones in the morning
questions.explain: IV insulin has a very short half-life. To avoid rebound DKA, overlap with subcutaneous insulin — stop the IV drip ~4 h after the first SC basal dose (or ~2 h after rapid-acting).

cards.prompt: Contrast the core defect in T1DM vs T2DM.
cards.answer: T1DM = autoimmune β-cell destruction → absolute insulin deficiency (ketosis-prone). T2DM = insulin resistance + progressive β-cell failure → relative deficiency.

cards.prompt: What does HbA1c reflect and over what period?
cards.answer: A weighted non-enzymatic glycation average of blood glucose over the prior 2–3 months (the ~120-day RBC lifespan).

cards.prompt: Name conditions that falsely LOWER HbA1c.
cards.answer: Anything shortening RBC lifespan: hemolytic anemia, sickle cell, thalassemia, recent transfusion. (Conditions lengthening RBC life — B12/folate deficiency, splenectomy — falsely raise it.)

cards.prompt: Why is metformin first-line, and its two key safety limits?
cards.answer: ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis, weight-neutral, no monotherapy hypoglycemia. Limits: B12 malabsorption + lactic acidosis; caution eGFR <60, stop <30.

cards.prompt: What organ protection do SGLT2 inhibitors give, and their signature risk?
cards.answer: CV (MACE), heart-failure, and renal protection plus weight loss; signature risk is euglycemic DKA (plus genital mycotic infections, volume depletion).

cards.prompt: How do GLP-1 receptor agonists lower glucose and weight?
cards.answer: Glucose-dependent insulin release, ↓ glucagon, slowed gastric emptying, and central satiety → potent A1c lowering + weight loss; some reduce MACE.

cards.prompt: Mechanism and main harms of sulfonylureas?
cards.answer: Close β-cell K_ATP channels → insulin exocytosis. Harms: hypoglycemia + weight gain; glyburide is worst (long-acting, renal active metabolites).

cards.prompt: How much creatinine rise is acceptable after starting an ACEi?
cards.answer: Up to ~30% from baseline within ~2 weeks is expected (reflects lower intraglomerular pressure) and is not a reason to stop, provided K+ is acceptable.

cards.prompt: Which diabetic patients get a statin, and to what LDL target?
cards.answer: All with diabetes ≥40 (or macrovascular disease/end-organ damage), regardless of baseline LDL; target LDL < 2.0 mmol/L or ≥50% reduction.

cards.prompt: When must you withhold insulin in DKA?
cards.answer: When serum K+ < 3.3 mmol/L — replace potassium first; insulin would drive K+ intracellularly and risk fatal arrhythmia.

cards.prompt: What endpoint guides the DKA insulin infusion rate?
cards.answer: Closure of the anion gap (clearing ketoacids) — not the blood glucose. Add D5W when glucose normalizes so insulin can continue.

cards.prompt: What distinguishes proliferative from non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy?
cards.answer: PDR has neovascularization (VEGF-driven) risking pre-retinal hemorrhage/tractional detachment; NPDR shows microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, exudates, cotton-wool spots.

cards.prompt: How is diabetic peripheral neuropathy screened?
cards.answer: Annual 10-g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament (loss of protective sensation) or 128-Hz vibration tuning fork on the great toe.

cards.prompt: Why is there little ketosis in HHS despite extreme hyperglycemia?
cards.answer: Residual endogenous insulin (T2DM) is enough to suppress lipolysis/ketogenesis but insufficient to control glucose → hyperosmolar non-ketotic state.

cards.prompt: The fasting plasma glucose threshold for diabetes is:
The HbA1c threshold to diagnose diabetes is:
SGLT2 inhibitors act primarily at the:
Metformin is contraindicated when eGFR falls below:
Which DPP-4 inhibitor needs no renal dose adjustment?
The dry cough of ACE inhibitors is mediated by accumulation of:
Which combination most increases rhabdomyolysis risk?
Before starting insulin in DKA you must check:
Euglycemic DKA is most characteristically precipitated by:
The earliest laboratory sign of diabetic nephropathy is:
Which class gives the most weight loss as a second agent?
Select the effects/risks of SGLT2 inhibitors:
Select absolute contraindications to ACE inhibitors:
Which agents carry meaningful hypoglycemia risk?
Select components of the DKA biochemical triad:
Select recognised diabetic microvascular complications:

cards.text: Anion gap = Na⁺ − ({{Cl⁻}} + {{HCO₃⁻}}); a value ≥ {{12}} signals unmeasured organic acids.
cards.explain: Acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate are the unmeasured anions in DKA.

cards.text: Pre-diabetes is defined by an HbA1c range of {{6.0}}% to {{6.4}}%.
cards.explain: At/above 6.5% is diabetes; 6.0–6.4% is the pre-diabetes band.

cards.text: Vascular-protection targets: A1c ≤ {{7.0}}%, BP < {{130/80}} mmHg, LDL < {{2.0}} mmol/L.
cards.explain: The ABCDES checklist drives cardiorenal risk reduction in diabetes.

cards.text: In DKA, when glucose falls to ~12–14 mmol/L but the gap is open, add {{D5W}} and continue the {{insulin}} infusion.
cards.explain: The goal is to keep clearing ketoacids without causing hypoglycemia.

cards.text: {{Linagliptin}} is cleared non-renally, so it needs no dose adjustment even in {{ESRD}}.
cards.explain: Useful in advanced CKD where other agents are restricted.

cards.prompt: Order the steps of DKA management:
items: Start IV insulin infusion, titrate to gap closure
Add D5W when glucose ~12–14 with open gap
Overlap with SC insulin and stop the IV drip

cards.prompt: Order a typical glycemic escalation in T2DM:
items: Add an agent matched to comorbidity (SGLT2/GLP-1)

cards.prompt: Order the diagnostic pathway for an asymptomatic abnormal glucose:
items: Repeat a confirmatory test on another day

cards.prompt: Match the drug to its mechanism:
Match the drug to its characteristic adverse effect:
Match the clinical priority to the best second agent:
Match the emergency to its hallmark:
How do you transition from IV to SC insulin in resolving DKA?
cards.answer: Overlap them: stop the IV infusion ~4 h after the first SC long-acting basal dose (or ~2 h after rapid-acting), once the patient is alert, eating, and the gap is closed.

cards.prompt: What drives gestational diabetes?
cards.answer: Placental hormones create an insulin-resistant state that unmasks glucose intolerance during pregnancy.

cards.prompt: How does low-dose ASA work and when is it used in diabetes?
cards.answer: Irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1 → no thromboxane A2 for the platelet's 7–10 day life. Used for secondary prevention (established CV disease), not routine primary prevention (bleeding risk).

cards.prompt: What is distinctive about tirzepatide?
cards.answer: A dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist with very potent A1c lowering and weight loss, and no specific renal dose adjustment.

cards.prompt: Which insulins cover meals vs provide basal coverage?
cards.answer: Rapid-acting analogues (lispro, aspart, glulisine) cover meals; long-acting analogues (glargine, detemir, degludec) give peakless basal coverage. NPH is intermediate and peaks (nocturnal hypoglycemia risk).

cards.text: A starting insulin regimen is ~{{0.5}} u/kg/day, split roughly {{50}}% basal and {{50}}% bolus; titrate basal by the {{fasting}} glucose.
cards.explain: Basal controls overnight/fasting glucose; bolus covers meals.

cards.prompt: Which scenario most clearly mandates insulin in T2DM?
How should the HbA1c target be individualized?
cards.answer: ≤7.0% for most; ≤6.5% if achievable safely in young, low-risk patients; loosen to 7.1–8.5% in the frail, hypoglycemia-prone, or those with limited life expectancy.

cards.prompt: How do you treat hypoglycemia in a conscious vs an unconscious patient?
cards.answer: Conscious: 15 g fast carbohydrate, recheck in 15 min, repeat (rule of 15). Unconscious/no swallow: IV dextrose, or IM/intranasal glucagon if no IV access.

cards.prompt: Which agents most commonly cause hypoglycemia?
What is Charcot foot and how is it managed?
cards.answer: A warm, swollen, red, often painless deformed foot in a neuropathic patient (mimics infection/gout). Off-load and immobilize early to prevent bony collapse.

cards.prompt: The leading cause of death in diabetes is:
Select factors that combine to cause diabetic foot ulcers:

guides.title: Week 25 · 1 Diabetes: Physiology, Classification & Diagnosis
guides.html:
Foundations

Diabetes: Physiology, Classification & Diagnosis

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Hormone classes, the pathophysiology of each diabetes type, what HbA1c really measures (and what fools it), and the diagnostic thresholds.

Hormone signalling — the big three classes

ClassExamplesReceptor / action
Protein / peptide (hydrophilic)Insulin, glucagon, TSH, ACTH, PTH, GH, prolactinCannot cross the membrane → bind cell-surface receptors (tyrosine kinase, GPCR, JAK-STAT) → second messengers
Steroid (lipophilic)Cortisol, aldosterone, testosterone, estradiolCross the membrane freely → intracellular/nuclear receptors act as transcription factors
Amino-acid derivativeThyroxine, epinephrine, norepinephrine, melatoninFrom tyrosine/tryptophan; mixed behaviour

Pathophysiology by type

  • Type 1 (T1DM): T-cell–mediated autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells → absolute insulin deficiency. Associated with other autoimmune disease; prone to DKA.
  • Type 2 (T2DM): peripheral insulin resistance + compensatory hyperinsulinemia, then progressive β-cell secretory failure → relative insulin deficiency. The main driver of rising prevalence in older adults is an age-related rise in insulin resistance.
  • Gestational (GDM): glucose intolerance first unmasked in pregnancy by placental insulin-resistant hormones.

HbA1c — what it measures and what fools it

HbA1c is a weighted, non-enzymatic glycation average over the prior 2–3 months (the ~120-day RBC lifespan).

HbA1c is unreliable when RBC turnover is abnormal
Anything that shortens RBC lifespan (hemolytic anemia, sickle cell, thalassemia, recent transfusion) falsely lowers A1c. Anything that lengthens RBC life or delays clearance (B12/folate deficiency, splenectomy, some iron deficiency) falsely raises it. In these patients use fasting glucose / OGTT instead.

Diagnostic thresholds

TestDiabetesPre-diabetes
Fasting plasma glucose≥ 7.0 mmol/L6.1–6.9 (IFG)
HbA1c≥ 6.5%6.0–6.4%
2-h 75 g OGTT≥ 11.1 mmol/L7.8–11.0 (IGT)
Random glucose + symptoms≥ 11.1 mmol/L—
In an asymptomatic patient a diagnosis needs a confirmatory repeat test (same or different assay) on another day. A single result + classic symptoms (or a hyperglycemic crisis) is enough on its own.

guides.title: Week 25 · 2 Diabetes Pharmacology
guides.html:
The matrix

Diabetes Pharmacology

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Six non-insulin classes — mechanism, the benefit that picks them, the harm that rules them out, and the renal cut-offs that get tested.

How to choose after metformin

Once metformin is on board, the second agent is chosen by the comorbidity: cardiovascular/renal disease → SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 agonist (organ-protective); weight loss the goal → GLP-1 agonist; cost/simplicity in the frail elderly → DPP-4 inhibitor; hypoglycemia must be avoided → avoid sulfonylureas.

The six classes

Class / drugMechanismPicks itRules it out
Biguanide — metformin↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis; ↑ peripheral uptake1st-line; weight-neutral; no hypoglycemiaGI upset, B12 malabsorption; lactic acidosis. Caution eGFR <60, stop <30
SGLT2 inhibitor — empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozinBlock proximal-tubule glucose reabsorption → glucosuriaCV + HF + renal protection; weight loss; mild diuresisGenital mycotic infection, volume depletion, euglycemic DKA; limited efficacy at low eGFR
GLP-1 agonist — semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide (dual: tirzepatide)Glucose-dependent insulin, ↓ glucagon, slows gastric emptying, central satietyPotent A1c + weight loss; MACE reduction (liraglutide, semaglutide)Nausea/vomiting, pancreatitis (rare); avoid in active proliferative retinopathy
DPP-4 inhibitor — sitagliptin, saxagliptin, linagliptinBlock incretin breakdown (↑ endogenous GLP-1/GIP)Weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia, well tolerated; good for frail elderlyModest efficacy; rare pancreatitis. Linagliptin = no renal dose adjustment
Sulfonylurea — gliclazide, glyburide, glimepirideClose β-cell KATP channel → insulin exocytosisRapid, potent, cheapHypoglycemia + weight gain; glyburide worst; avoid in frail/elderly
α-glucosidase inhibitor — acarboseDelay intestinal carbohydrate breakdownTargets post-prandial glucoseFlatulence, bloating, diarrhea; weak A1c effect

Renal cut-offs worth memorizing

  • Metformin: caution <60, contraindicated <30 mL/min/1.73m² (lactic acidosis).
  • Sitagliptin: 50 mg if eGFR 30–60, 25 mg if <30. Linagliptin: non-renal clearance → no adjustment, safe in ESRD.
  • Sulfonylureas: stop standard agents <50; gliclazide is safer but stop <30. Glyburide has the longest action + renally-cleared active metabolites → highest prolonged-hypoglycemia risk.
  • Tirzepatide: no specific renal dose adjustment.
Pattern for exams: weight loss wanted → GLP-1; HF or CKD → SGLT2; avoid hypoglycemia in the elderly → DPP-4 (linagliptin if renal); severe hyperglycemia + acute illness or low eGFR → insulin.

guides.title: Week 25 · 3 Vascular Protection & Comorbid Pharmacology
guides.html:
ABCDES

Vascular Protection & Comorbid Pharmacology

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Diabetes is a vascular disease — the ABCDES checklist plus the ACEi creatinine rule, statin targets, and antiplatelet pearls.

The ABCDES of vascular protection

LetterTarget / action
A — A1c≤ 7.0% for most
B — Blood pressure< 130/80 mmHg
C — CholesterolLDL < 2.0 mmol/L (or ≥50% reduction)
D — Drugs for cardiorenal protectionACEi/ARB, statin, ± SGLT2/GLP-1, ± ASA
E — Exercise / EatingLifestyle
S — Smoking cessationStop

ACE inhibitors & ARBs

ACEi (ramipril, perindopril, lisinopril, enalapril) block angiotensin II, dilate the efferent arteriole, and lower intraglomerular pressure — renoprotective in diabetes, especially with albuminuria.

The 30% creatinine rule
A creatinine rise of up to 30% from baseline within ~2 weeks of starting an ACEi/ARB is expected (it reflects the intended drop in intraglomerular pressure) and is not a reason to stop — provided potassium is acceptable. Recheck creatinine + K+. Only stop for a rise >30%, hyperkalemia, or AKI.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy (teratogenic), prior angioedema, hyperkalemia, bilateral renal-artery stenosis.
  • ACEi cough (bradykinin) → switch to an ARB (candesartan, irbesartan, valsartan). Do not combine ACEi + ARB.

Statins

All patients with diabetes who are ≥ 40 (or have macrovascular disease / end-organ damage) get a statin, regardless of baseline LDL. Atorvastatin/rosuvastatin inhibit HMG-CoA reductase → up-regulate hepatic LDL receptors. Target LDL < 2.0 mmol/L.

  • AEs: myalgia (commonest reason for stopping), transient LFT rise; rare rhabdomyolysis.
  • Interaction: atorvastatin is CYP3A4-metabolized — macrolides/azoles raise levels and myopathy risk; rosuvastatin has fewer CYP3A4 interactions.
  • Fibrates are reserved for severe hypertriglyceridemia (pancreatitis prevention), not LDL lowering; ezetimibe is an add-on, not first-line.

Antiplatelet (ASA)

Low-dose ASA (81 mg) irreversibly acetylates COX-1 → no thromboxane A2 for the platelet's 7–10 day life. Used for secondary prevention (established CV disease); not routine for primary prevention because of bleeding (dyspepsia, GI bleed, PUD).

Classic vignette: ACEi started, creatinine up ~28%, K+ normal → continue and monitor, do not stop or switch.

guides.title: Week 25 · 4 Microvascular Complications & Screening
guides.html:
Eyes, kidneys, nerves

Microvascular Complications & Screening

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Retinopathy, nephropathy and neuropathy — the pathophysiology, the screening tool, and the confirmation rules that get tested.

Retinopathy

Chronic hyperglycemia → basement-membrane thickening, pericyte drop-out, capillary nonperfusion → ischemia → VEGF → neovascularization.

NPDR (non-proliferative)PDR (proliferative)
Microaneurysms, dot-and-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spotsNeovascularization (disc/elsewhere) → pre-retinal hemorrhage, tractional detachment
  • Screen: dilated fundus exam. T2DM at diagnosis, then q1–2 yr. T1DM 5 years after onset (age ≥15), then annually.

Nephropathy

Earliest change: glomerular basement-membrane thickening, podocyte loss, mesangial expansion. Afferent dilation causes hyperfiltration, so eGFR can look normal/high before it declines.

Confirm albuminuria before you label it
Screen at diagnosis and annually with a random UACR + eGFR. Microalbuminuria (UACR 2.0–20 mg/mmol) is the first sign — but a single value never diagnoses nephropathy. Confirm 2 of 3 samples over ~3 months, because UTI, fever, vigorous exercise, decompensated HF, menstruation, and acute BP/glucose spikes all transiently raise UACR.

Neuropathy

  • Distal symmetric polyneuropathy: "glove-and-stocking" sensory loss from the feet up. Screen annually with a 10-g monofilament (loss of protective sensation) or 128-Hz tuning fork.
  • Autonomic: gastroparesis (early satiety, erratic glucose), resting tachycardia, erectile dysfunction, bladder retention, silent myocardial ischemia.
Loss of protective sensation on the monofilament = high ulcer/amputation risk → foot-care referral, daily inspection, proper footwear.

guides.title: Week 25 · 5 Hyperglycemic Emergencies: DKA & HHS
guides.html:
Crises

Hyperglycemic Emergencies: DKA & HHS

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Tell DKA from HHS, then run the DKA algorithm — potassium before insulin, treat the anion gap not the glucose, and the dextrose pivot.

DKA vs HHS

DKAHHS
Typical patientT1DM (or stressed T2DM)T2DM, often elderly
Glucose> 14 mmol/L (lower if on SGLT2)often > 30 mmol/L
Ketones / acidosisPositive; anion-gap acidosis (pH <7.30, HCO₃ <15)Minimal/none (HCO₃ ≥22, gap <12)
OsmolalityVariable> 320 mOsm/kg, profound dehydration
WhyAbsolute insulin lack → lipolysis + ketogenesisResidual insulin suppresses ketones but can't control glucose

Anion gap = Na+ − (Cl− + HCO₃−); ≥ 12 signals unmeasured organic acids (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate). Kussmaul breathing = respiratory compensation.

Euglycemic DKA

SGLT2 inhibitor + acute stress
Ongoing glucosuria keeps glucose near-normal while counter-regulatory hormones (glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine) drive unchecked ketogenesis. Anion-gap acidosis + ketones with a only-mildly-elevated glucose = euglycemic DKA. Hold the SGLT2 inhibitor during acute illness ("sick-day" rule).

DKA management — in order

StepWhy
1. Check K+ firstIf K+ < 3.3 → hold insulin, give IV KCl first. Hypokalemia is the leading avoidable cause of death. If 3.3–5.0, add KCl to fluids with insulin.
2. FluidsNormal saline 1–2 L/h initially; restores volume and itself lowers glucose.
3. Insulin infusionIV regular insulin ~0.1 u/kg/h. Titrate to anion-gap closure, not to glucose.
4. Dextrose pivotWhen glucose ~12–14 but gap still open, do not stop insulin — add D5W and keep the infusion going until ketoacids clear.
5. SC transitionOnly when alert, eating, gap closed: overlap IV with SC. Stop IV 4 h after first SC basal (or 2 h after rapid).
Two traps: giving insulin before fixing K+ <3.3, and stopping insulin when glucose normalizes while the gap is still open (ketoacidosis rebounds). Bicarbonate is almost never indicated.

guides.title: Week 25 · 6 Insulin Therapy
guides.html:
Insulin

Insulin Therapy

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The insulin types by onset/duration, how basal-bolus is built and titrated, and when type-2 patients actually need insulin.

Insulin preparations

TypeExamplesOnset / peak / durationRole
Rapid-actinglispro, aspart, glulisine~15 min / 1–2 h / 3–5 hPrandial (with meals); IV in some protocols
Short (regular)regular insulin~30 min / 2–3 h / 6–8 hIV infusion in DKA/HHS; older prandial
IntermediateNPH~2 h / 4–8 h / 12–18 hBasal (has a peak → nocturnal hypo risk)
Long-actingglargine, detemir, degludec~2 h / flat / 20–42 hBasal (peakless, steadier)
Pre-mixede.g. 70/30biphasicFixed regimens, adherence-limited patients

Building a regimen

  • Total daily dose ~0.5 u/kg/day to start; split roughly 50% basal / 50% bolus (bolus divided across meals).
  • Basal controls fasting/overnight glucose; bolus (prandial) covers meals; a correction ("sliding") scale adjusts for highs.
  • Titrate basal by the fasting glucose (e.g. +1–2 u every few days until fasting at target); titrate prandial by the next pre-meal/post-meal value.

When a type-2 patient needs insulin

Reach for insulin when
Symptomatic hyperglycemia with weight loss or ketosis; HbA1c very high (e.g. >10%) at presentation; a hyperglycemic emergency; severe intercurrent illness, surgery, or glucocorticoids; advanced renal/hepatic disease that limits oral agents; or pregnancy where oral options are inadequate. Insulin is the most titratable, organ-independent option.
  • Start with basal insulin added to oral agents (simplest); advance to basal-plus-one-bolus, then full basal-bolus if needed.
  • Continue metformin where appropriate (lowers the insulin dose and weight gain) unless contraindicated.
Rapid analogues for meals, a long-acting analogue for basal; NPH peaks and carries nocturnal-hypoglycemia risk. In DKA the IV agent is regular insulin, transitioned to subcutaneous basal with overlap.

guides.title: Week 25 · 7 Glycemic Targets, Monitoring & Hypoglycemia
guides.html:
Targets & lows

Glycemic Targets, Monitoring & Hypoglycemia

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Individualizing the A1c target, how glucose is monitored, and recognizing and treating hypoglycemia (including unawareness).

Individualizing the A1c target

PatientReasonable A1c target
Most adults with T2DM≤ 7.0%
Younger, short duration, low hypoglycemia risk≤ 6.5% (if achievable safely)
Frail, limited life expectancy, hypoglycemia-prone, recurrent severe lows7.1–8.5% (looser)

Tighter is not always better — over-treating the frail elderly causes more harm (falls, hypoglycemia) than the modest microvascular gain.

Monitoring tools

  • HbA1c every 3–6 months (remember the RBC-turnover pitfalls).
  • SMBG (fingerstick) — essential for anyone on insulin or secretagogues; timing matched to the regimen (fasting + pre-meal ± post-meal).
  • CGM — continuous interstitial glucose with time-in-range and trend arrows; reduces hypoglycemia, especially in T1DM and insulin-treated T2DM.

Hypoglycemia

Recognize and treat
Whipple's triad = low measured glucose + consistent symptoms + relief on correction. Adrenergic warning signs (tremor, sweating, palpitations, hunger) precede neuroglycopenia (confusion, seizures, coma). Highest-risk drugs: insulin and sulfonylureas.
  • Conscious / able to swallow: 15 g fast carbohydrate, recheck in 15 min, repeat ("rule of 15"), then a longer-acting snack.
  • Unconscious / cannot swallow: IV dextrose, or IM/intranasal glucagon if no IV access.
  • Hypoglycemia unawareness: repeated lows blunt the warning response → relax targets and avoid further lows for weeks to restore awareness.
  • Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia can be prolonged/recurrent — admit and observe (octreotide can help refractory cases).
A target is a tool, not a trophy: loosen it for the frail and the hypoglycemia-prone. Any insulin/sulfonylurea patient with confusion — check a glucose first.

guides.title: Week 25 · 8 Diabetic Foot & Macrovascular Disease
guides.html:
Feet & arteries

Diabetic Foot & Macrovascular Disease

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The at-risk foot, ulcer and Charcot recognition, and the macrovascular disease that actually kills patients with diabetes.

The diabetic foot

Ulcers arise from the combination of neuropathy (loss of protective sensation), peripheral arterial disease (poor healing), and deformity/pressure (callus, Charcot). Infection then complicates them.

AssessHow
Protective sensation10-g monofilament (4 sites) + 128-Hz tuning fork
PerfusionPedal pulses, ankle-brachial index; consider imaging if reduced
Deformity / pressureInspect for callus, clawing, prior ulcers, Charcot changes
Infection / depthProbe-to-bone (suggests osteomyelitis), erythema, discharge
Two not-to-miss
Osteomyelitis — a deep or non-healing ulcer that probes to bone; needs imaging and prolonged antibiotics ± surgery. Charcot foot — a warm, swollen, red, often painless foot with deformity in a neuropathic patient; off-load and immobilize early to prevent collapse (it mimics infection/gout).

Prevention

  • Annual foot screen; daily self-inspection; proper footwear; podiatry for high-risk feet; never walk barefoot.
  • Loss of protective sensation = high-risk foot → structured foot-care program.

Macrovascular disease

Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (MI, stroke, peripheral arterial disease) is the leading cause of death in diabetes. Risk reduction is the same ABCDES package plus organ-protective glucose-lowering:

  • Established ASCVD or high risk → prefer a GLP-1 agonist (atherosclerotic benefit) and/or an SGLT2 inhibitor (heart-failure/renal benefit) regardless of A1c.
  • Statin for essentially all; ACEi/ARB for hypertension/albuminuria; smoking cessation; antiplatelet for secondary prevention.
Microvascular complications (eyes/kidneys/nerves) cause morbidity; macrovascular disease causes mortality — which is why the organ-protective drugs are chosen by cardiovascular/renal status, not just glucose.

guides.title: Week 25 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Endocrine I — Diabetes

Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
DKA → check K⁺ FIRST (hold insulin if <3.3), fluids, insulin titrated to gap closure, D5W when glucose ~12–14
Euglycemic DKA (SGLT2 + acute illness) → anion-gap acidosis with near-normal glucose — treat as DKA, hold the SGLT2 inhibitor
HHS (glucose >30, osmolality >320, no ketones) → aggressive fluids; watch K⁺ and osmotic shifts
K⁺ < 3.3 in DKA → the leading avoidable death — replace potassium before any insulin
Creatinine ↑>30% or hyperkalemia after ACEi → stop/recheck (a rise ≤30% is expected, keep going)
Severe hyperglycemia + acute illness or eGFR <30 → insulin (stop metformin)

Diagnosis Dx

Fasting glucose ≥ 7.0 mmol/L
HbA1c ≥ 6.5% (pre-diabetes 6.0–6.4)
2-h OGTT ≥ 11.1 mmol/L
Asymptomatic needs a confirmatory repeat on another day
A1c falsely low hemolysis, sickle cell, thalassemia, transfusion
A1c falsely high B12/folate deficiency, splenectomy

Pick the agent Therapy

First-line metformin (weight-neutral, no hypoglycemia)
Weight loss GLP-1 agonist (semaglutide)
HF / CKD SGLT2 inhibitor
Frail elderly DPP-4 (linagliptin = no renal adjustment)
Avoid sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia + weight gain; glyburide worst)
Severe / acute / eGFR<30 insulin

Renal cut-offs Renal

Metformin caution <60, STOP <30
Sitagliptin 50 mg (30–60), 25 mg (<30)
Linagliptin no adjustment, safe in ESRD
Sulfonylureas stop <50; gliclazide safer, stop <30
SGLT2 limited efficacy at low eGFR

Vascular protection ABCDES

A1c / BP / LDL ≤7.0% / <130/80 / <2.0 mmol/L
ACEi creatinine ≤30% rise is expected — continue
ACEi cough switch to an ARB
Statin all diabetics ≥40 or with CVD
Statin interaction atorvastatin + macrolide/azole → myopathy
ASA secondary prevention, not routine primary

Complications & screening Micro

Retinopathy T2DM at dx then q1–2 yr; T1DM 5 yr after onset
PDR hallmark VEGF-driven neovascularization
Nephropathy random UACR; confirm 2 of 3 over ~3 mo
Microalbuminuria UACR 2–20 mg/mmol = earliest sign
Neuropathy 10-g monofilament / 128-Hz fork annually
Autonomic gastroparesis, silent ischemia, ED

DKA vs HHS Crises

DKA ketones +, gap acidosis, pH <7.30
HHS osmolality >320, minimal ketosis, T2DM elderly
DKA endpoint close the gap, not the glucose
Dextrose pivot add D5W, keep insulin going
SC transition overlap; stop IV 4 h after basal / 2 h after rapid

Insulin Insulin

Rapid (lispro/aspart) prandial; ~15 min onset
Long (glargine/degludec) basal; peakless
NPH intermediate, peaks → nocturnal hypo
Regimen ~0.5 u/kg; 50% basal / 50% bolus
Titrate basal by the fasting glucose
T2DM needs insulin A1c very high, ketosis, illness, pregnancy

Targets & lows Targets

Most adults A1c ≤ 7.0%
Frail / hypoglycemia-prone loosen to 7.1–8.5%
Whipple's triad low glucose + symptoms + relief on correction
Rule of 15 15 g carb, recheck 15 min, repeat
No IV access IM/intranasal glucagon
Highest-risk drugs insulin, sulfonylureas

Foot & macrovascular Foot/CVD

Ulcer triad neuropathy + PAD + pressure/deformity
Probe-to-bone suggests osteomyelitis
Charcot foot warm, swollen, painless → off-load early
Leading cause of death macrovascular (ASCVD)
ASCVD prefer GLP-1; HF/CKD prefer SGLT2
Screen annual foot exam + daily self-inspection
Week 25 · Endocrine I — Diabetes · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.adverse: GI upset, B12 malabsorption; lactic acidosis (rare)
Genital mycotic infection, volume depletion, euglycemic DKA

facets.mechanism: GLP-1 receptor agonist (incretin mimetic; central satiety)
facets.contra: Active proliferative retinopathy; caution advanced CKD

facets.mechanism: Sulfonylurea — closes β-cell K_ATP channel → insulin release

facets.adverse: Highest prolonged-hypoglycemia risk (long-acting, renal active metabolites); avoid in elderly/CKD

facets.monitoring: Titrate to anion-gap closure; check K+ before starting

facets.use: Basal insulin; SC transition out of DKA
facets.monitoring: Overlap with IV insulin before stopping the drip

facets.use: LDL lowering / CV protection in diabetes

facets.mechanism: Irreversible COX-1 inhibition → no thromboxane A2

facets.use: Severe hypoglycemia when no IV access
